rulings.Case Title: KIERAN BUCKLEY et al VS. DAVID BARTENWERFER et al
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 27, 2012, Line 2, DEFENDANT OCCIDENTAL EXPRESS' MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Denied. While defendants have met their initial burden, Plaintiffs have met their shifting burden to show that there are triable issues of fact whether Occidental Express exceeded its role as a permit expediter in the construction project at 549 28th Street, San Francisco, California. H. Karnilowicz testified he signed permits as general contractor, and there is evidence that defendants "subbed out work". Expert Kneppers testified defendants managed some of the work, and emails between Karnilowicz and Bartenwerfer suggest the same. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g), must indicate any objections to the cited evidence are overruled, and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/CK)

rulings.Case Title: NETTA GRUTMAN VS. REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA et al
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 27, 2012, Line 4, DEFENDANTs REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA, TODD MCGREGOR, JAMES JACOBS' MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION. Defendants' motion is granted. The motion is brought under C.C.P. § 437c(s), and is bound by the stipulation and order previously entered ("Stipulation"). Plaintiff contends that a motion arguing defendants' immunity is improper under the Stipulation. The Court disagrees: immunity is one reason for the defendant's opposition to plaintiff's position that she is entitled to a certain minimum recovery, which entitlement is a subject of the Stipulation. On the merits, plaintiff cannot recover damages under Civil Code § 52(a) against defendants. Gov't Code § 818 proscribes the recovery of punitive damages against a public entity such as the UC Regents and plaintiff cannot recover punitive damages against the individuals because the undisputed evidence demonstrates that they were acting in their official capacities. Jefferson v. City of Fremont, 2012 U.S. Dist. LEXIS 60141 at *7-8 (N.D.Cal. 2012); Harris v. Capital Growth Investors XIV, 52 Cal.3d 1142, 1172 (1991), overruled on other grounds, Munson v. del Taco, Inc., 46 Cal.4th 661 (2009). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/CK)

rulings.Case Title: MARCIA GIUSTI et al VS. BKCM ENTERPRISES INC., A CALIFORNIA CORPORAION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn To Compel Further Responses To Special Interrogatories And Req. For Admissions And Req. For Monetary Sanctions

The Court adopts the recommendation of Pro Tem Thornley in part as follows: GRANTED in part, DENIED in part. Special Interrogatories: # 35, 36, 37 DENIED; objection sustained; requests contain improper subparts. # 53, 81 DENIED. The responding party states these are all the facts now known. rulings.Case Title: MARC E BEJARANO VS. MICHELLE M GAGNON et al
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 27, 2012, Line 7, DEFENDANT THE BOARD OF TRUSTEES OF CALIFORNIA STATE's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Denied. There exists a triable issue of material fact as to whether Defendant Gagnon was acting within the scope of her employment at the time of the accident. See generally, Gipson v. Davis Realty, 215 Cal.App.2d 190 (1963)(generally issue of fact). The jury may infer Gagnon was not commuting, but travelling between offices at the behest of the employer. Work hours were between 9 am and 6 pm (UMF 10) and the employer apparently required this travel. E.g. Gagnon Depo. at 31, 34, 35. Even where facts are undisputed conflicting inferences may be in some cases be drawn, and that is the case here. In such a situation the court is required to favor the party resisting summary judgment. E.g., Brassinga v. City of Mountain View, 66 Cal. App. 4th 195, 210 (1998). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. This directive does not preclude plaintiff from presenting a proposed order which in addition includes other language such as that in the proposal presented with the papers. =(302/CK)

rulings.Case Title: LEONARD M. GEYER VS. ROBERT DREYER et al
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 27, 2012, Line 14, DEFENDANTs ROBERT DREYER, ROBERT DREYER INC, ACCOUNTANT, A CORPORATION, CHRISTINA DREYER INC, ACCOUNTANT, A CORPORATION, DAVID LUCHETTI, DAVID LUCHETTI INC, ACCOUNTANT, A CORPORATION, DREYER, GEYER, LUCHETTI INC., ACCOUNTANT, A CORPORATION, CHRISTINA DREYER's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Sustained with ten days leave to amend. As to the fifth cause of action, the § 17200 claim does not adequately allege any unlawful or fraudulent business practice. Plaintiff has failed to allege any specific law 'borrowed' for the "unlawful" prong of § 17200. To assert the "fraudulent business practice" prong, Plaintiff must show actual reliance on the specified alleged misrepresentation, as well as the specificity referred to below. Plaintiff has failed to allege reliance. Plaintiff is granted leave to amend, if he can do so in good faith, to cure the defects. As to the seventh cause of action for fraud, the elements that must be pleaded with particularity include: (1) misrepresentation; (2) knowledge of falsity; (3) intent to defraud; (4) justifiable reliance; and (5) resulting damage. Philipson & Simon v. Gulsvig (2007) 154 CA4th 347, 363. Plaintiff has failed to plead facts with sufficient particularity as to each element for the fraud claim. Plaintiff is granted leave to amend. The Court does not contemplate that further leave to amend will be granted. rulings.Case Title: RAHMON MOMOH VS. CALIFORNIA PUBLIC UTILITIES COMMISSION et al
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 27, 2012, Line 15, DEFENDANT CALIFORNIA PUBLIC UTILITIES COMMISSION'S DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. The request for judicial notice is denied. The proposed papers are irrelevant to the pending motion. The demurrers are sustained in part. The demurrer is sustained with ten days leave to amend as to the first, second, third, fourth, fifth, sixth, and ninth causes of action. As to the first, second, third, fourth, fifth, and sixth causes of action, Plaintiff is granted leave to amend, if he can do so in good faith, to allege circumstances that support the inference that Defendant's actions were based on race, color, or national origin discrimination. Plaintiff may be under a misapprehension of the prima facie elements: The fourth element is not precisely as stated in the Opposition at 2 (person not in protected class selected). "A prima facie case generally means the plaintiff must provide evidence that (1) the plaintiff was a member of a protected class, (2) the plaintiff was qualified for the position he or she sought or was performing competently in the position held, (3) the plaintiff suffered an adverse employment action, such as termination, demotion, or denial of an available job, and (4) some other circumstance suggests a discriminatory motive." Scotch v. Art Institute of California, 173 Cal. App. 4th 986, 1004 (2009). As to the ninth cause of action, the demurrer is sustained with 10 days leave to amend per Plaintiff's request in his opposition papers. The demurrer is otherwise overruled. Plaintiff has adequately alleged that Plaintiff was discriminated against based on his age and that Defendant's promotional practices have a disparate impact on employees over forty years old. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: DAVID M FOLMER VS. CITY OF SAN JOSE, MINETA AIRPORT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Friday, July 27, 2012, Line 17, DEFENDANT CITY OF SAN JOSE'S DEMURRER TO COMPLAINT. Sustained without leave to amend. The Court notes that venue does not appear to lie in this Court, but it also appears that defendant has waived this basis, at least at this time. The complaint is incoherent, and thus the demurrer for uncertainty is sustained. While such a ruling normally includes leave to amend, at least on an initial demurrer, leave to amend will not be granted because there are other, and fatal, bases requiring the court to sustain the demurrer. To the extent that Plaintiff attempts to state causes of action for intentional tort, attempted intentional tort, conspiracy, insurance fraud, and stalking, there is no statutory basis for these causes of action and Defendant is therefore immune from liability. Govt. Code § 815; Peterson v. San Francisco Community College Dist. (1984) 36 Cal.3d 799, 809. To the extent that Plaintiff attempts to state a cause of action for obstruction of justice, this cause of action fails because obstruction of justice is a criminal act., Pen. Code § 135, and no civil right claim can be predicated upon a mere violation of a criminal statute. Agnew v. Parks (1959) 172 Cal.App.2d 756, 765. Plaintiff's claim for overtime pay that he was allegedly denied between January 13, 2003 and October 2006 (Complaint, p. 8) is time-barred by the 3-year statute of limitations in CCP § 338(a). Plaintiff's causes of action for harassment and retaliation under FEHA are barred because Plaintiff failed to timely exhaust his administrative remedies. The timely filing of an administrative complaint, and exhaustion of that remedy, are prerequisites to maintenance of an action for damages under the FEHA. Balloon v. Superior Court, (1995) 39 Cal.App.4th 1116, 1120, superseded on other grounds; Accardi v. Superior Court (1993) 17 Cal.App.4th 341, 349; Denney v. Universal City Studios, Inc. (1992) 10 Cal.App.4th 1226, 1232; Valdez v. City of Los Angeles (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 1043, 1052. An administrative complaint must be filed no later than one year from the date upon which the alleged unlawful practice occurred. Govt. Code § 12960(d). Plaintiff resigned from his position with Defendant in November 2007. Complaint, p. 3. Although Plaintiff alleges retaliatory and harassing acts that occurred after his employment ended, such conduct is not actionable. Plaintiff did not file an administrative complaint until April 2011. Complaint, p. 2. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/CK)
